## Referee report on the revised draft

### Verdict

The revised `answer.tex` **now appears mathematically complete and answer-ready**. The author has addressed the two prior fatal issues:

1. the invalid strong-operator-to-universal-bidual weak-* passage; and  
2. the invalid multiplication of concrete \(B(L^2M)\)-operators inside the nonmultiplicative DKEP normal bidual.

The new proof of the boundary support intersection works in the ordinary enveloping bidual \(A^{**}\), uses concrete coefficient products only there, and passes to the normal bidual only after applying the DKEP support projection. This is the correct strategy in view of DKEP Remark 2.8, which explicitly notes that the canonical map \(A\to(A^\sharp)^*\) is generally not multiplicative. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

## LaTeX contract check

I compiled the supplied `answer.tex` twice with `pdflatex`.

- Document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- The `fullpage` package is used; no forbidden layout package or manual margin change is present.
- No forbidden line-spacing or in-document font-size changes are present.
- Compilation succeeds.
- The PDF is 5 pages, within the 12-page limit.

So there is **no LaTeX-contract violation**.

---

## Literature validation

The revised proof’s main cited inputs are supported by the literature:

- DKEP’s normal-bidual framework, including Proposition 2.10 and the warning that the canonical embedding into the normal bidual is not generally multiplicative, matches the revised proof’s caution and ordinary-bidual strategy. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.6 supports the annihilator criterion used to pass from \(K_X^{\infty,1}\)-membership to vanishing against \(\sharp_J\)-normal functionals that vanish on \(X\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Theorem 5.10, Proposition 5.9, and Example 5.11 support the direct-sum/mixing argument and the coefficient-boundary formalism. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- DKEP Lemma 8.5 supports the final normal-bidual characterization of proper proximality relative to a boundary piece. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))
- CDHJKN Theorem 0.4 / Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 support the graph-product correspondence decomposition and fusion rules used in the boundary-intersection lemma. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743?nt=1))
- CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 and Proposition 6.3 support the final factoriality and nonamenability conclusions. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743?nt=1))

---

## Review of previous concerns

### 1. Nonmultiplicativity of the normal bidual — addressed

The previous draft incorrectly multiplied concrete coefficient operators after embedding them into \(\mathcal A_M=(B(L^2M)^{\sharp_J})^*\). The revised proof explicitly avoids this. In the new boundary-support intersection lemma, all coefficient products \(hkh\) are computed concretely in \(B(L^2M)\), then limits are taken in the ordinary bidual \(A_M^{**}\), and only afterward is the result compressed by the DKEP projection \(p_M\).

This resolves the earlier fatal objection.

### 2. Strong-operator convergence versus weak-* convergence in \(A^{**}\) — addressed

The old proof used strong convergence of finite-coordinate cutdowns to infer weak-* convergence in the universal bidual, which was invalid. The revised direct-sum lemma instead uses the DKEP mixing-closure argument from the proof of Theorem 5.10. This is the right mechanism and avoids the earlier topology error.

### 3. Induced subalgebra boundary conventions — addressed

The revised proof of \(X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B\) fixes the earlier directional confusion for the coefficient maps \(T_{\widehat 1}\). It now correctly identifies \(T_{\widehat1}:L^2N\to L^2B\) for \({}_NL^2N_B\) and the inclusion \(T_{\widehat1}:L^2B\to L^2N\) for \({}_BL^2N_N\). This section is now acceptable.

### 4. Hypertrace step in the finite-boundary criterion — addressed

The finite-boundary criterion now treats \(\Theta(T)=q_0Tq_0\) as DKEP’s u.c.p. compression into the normal compact corner, not as a multiplicative embedding. The construction of the resulting Connes hypertrace is sufficiently clear.

---

## Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### Problem interpretation

The interpretation of irreducibility as nontrivial join-irreducibility, equivalently connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\), is explicitly stated. This resolves the ambiguity in the original problem statement.

### Normal supports and coefficient boundary pieces

The normal-support paragraph is now correctly framed around the ordinary bidual \(A^{**}\) and the support projection \(p_N\). The proof should be read for the boundary pieces actually used later, namely \(X_{M_U}\), \(X_B\), and \(\mathbb K(L^2M)\), for which the necessary multiplier hypotheses hold; DKEP Example 3.4 explicitly gives \(M\) and \(JMJ\) in the multiplier algebra for subalgebra boundary pieces. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

### Lemma `\ref{lem:coeff}`

The proof of \(X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B\) is now coherent. The fusion argument gives \(X_{\mathcal H_B}\subset X_B\), while the vectors \(\xi\otimes\widehat1\) show that every generator of \(X_B\) appears as a coefficient for \(\mathcal H_B\). The self-conjugacy map in the tracial case is standard.

### Lemma `\ref{lem:dsupport}`

The direct-sum support argument now uses DKEP’s strong-module closure machinery rather than invalid universal-bidual weak-* convergence. The conclusion that coefficients of the direct sum lie in \(K_X(N)\) follows from mixing relative to \(X\times B(L^2N)\), Theorem 5.10, and Proposition 5.9.

The construction of \(\mathcal C_U\) and the approximate units is also valid: coefficient products are computed concretely using \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\), and functional calculus with functions vanishing at zero gives contractive approximate units inside the required norm closure.

### Lemma `\ref{lem:intersection}`

This is the critical repair, and it now works.

For positive finite-fusion coefficients \(h=T_\xi^*T_\xi\) and \(k=T_\eta^*T_\eta\), the identity

\[
hkh=T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}^*
T_{\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta}
\]

is used only in the concrete algebra \(B(L^2M)\). CDHJKN’s fusion rule then decomposes the correspondence containing \(\xi\otimes\bar\xi\otimes\eta\) over subsets of \(U\cap W\). The direct-sum lemma places this coefficient in \(K_{X_{M_{U\cap W}}}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Passing to approximate-unit limits in \(A^{**}\), then using annihilators in \(A^{\sharp_J}\), proves

\[
0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]

The induction producing the palindromic support inclusion is also correct.

### Finite boundary criterion

The compression by \(q_i^\perp\) is valid because the DKEP support projections commute with both \(N\) and \(JNJ\). The GNS argument with the positive palindromic product is correct. The construction of the hypertrace from \(\omega(q_0)=1\) is now adequately justified.

### Relative Bass–Serre paradox

The reduced-word arguments are concise but mathematically standard. The projections \(p,q,e_B\) lie in \(S_{X_B}(N)\), the inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]

follow from the AFP reduced-word decomposition, and the \(B\)-Haar unitary \(h=w_1a\) gives pairwise orthogonal translates of \(e_B\). This proves relative proper proximality via DKEP Theorem 6.2.

### Application to graph products

The graph-product AFP splitting is standard. The construction of \(w_1,w_2\) from the complement graph is correct in both cases \(|C(v)|\ge2\) and \(|C(v)|=1\). The intersection of all links is empty because no vertex lies in its own link. The boundary-intersection lemma then places the palindromic product in the compact corner.

The final use of CDHJKN to obtain factoriality and nonamenability is valid: connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) with at least three vertices rules out universal vertices and isolated exceptional complement edges, and Proposition 6.3 rules out amenability under the same condition.

---

## Final assessment

The revised proof now resolves the earlier fatal defects. I do not find a remaining mathematical gap that affects the stated theorem. The document compiles, satisfies the LaTeX contract, records the problem interpretation, and gives a complete proof under that interpretation.

# Problem statement

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

# Author's solution attempt

## `answer.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}

\DeclareMathOperator{\Lk}{Lk}
\newcommand{\bbarotimes}{\mathbin{\overline{\otimes}}}
\newcommand{\cA}{\mathcal A}
\newcommand{\cH}{\mathcal H}
\newcommand{\cK}{\mathcal K}
\newcommand{\cL}{\mathcal L}
\newcommand{\cF}{\mathcal F}
\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb C}

\begin{document}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I use the standard graph-product meaning of irreducible: the finite graph
\(\Gamma=(V,E)\) is not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement
\(\Gamma^c\) is connected.  The traces are faithful normal traces.  For
\(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical expectation.

Put \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
for the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal bidual of
Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson (DKEP) \cite{DKEP}.  If \(X\) is an
\(N\)-boundary piece, \(q_X\in\cA_N\) denotes the support projection of the
corner \((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  Thus
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=
 \{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\) and \(\widetilde S(N)\).  We use DKEP
Lemma 8.5 for the trivial group action: if \(N\) is a separable finite factor,
then proper proximality relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the absence of an
\(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) restricting to the trace.

\section*{Normal supports and graph-product boundary corners}

Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  If \(D\subset A\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra
invariant under the multiplier actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), let
\(s_D\in A^{**}\) be its open support.  In each one-normal category, the
paragraph before DKEP Remark 2.8 identifies the normal bidual of \(D\) with
\(s_D(A^\sharp)^*s_D\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 identifies simultaneous normal
biduals with intersections of the two one-normal biduals; hence
\[
        (D^{\sharp_J})^*=\cA_N\cap s_DA^{**}s_D                  \tag{1}
\]
inside \(A^{**}\).  This is a corner of \(\cA_N\); denote its unit by \(q(D)\).
Thus \(T\in q(D)\cA_Nq(D)\) exactly when \(T\in\cA_N\) and \(T=s_DTs_D\) in
\(A^{**}\).

For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\), \(q_X=q(K_X(N))\), but this is also \(q(X)\).
Indeed, if \(\phi\in A^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X\), then, since \(XAX\) is
norm dense in the hereditary algebra \(X\), DKEP Theorem 5.6 with \(X=Y\) gives
\(\phi(T)=0\) for every \(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), hence for every
\(T\in K_X(N)\).  The converse is trivial from \(X\subset K_X(N)\).  Thus \(X\)
and \(K_X(N)\) have the same annihilator in \(A^{\sharp_J}\) and the same
weak-* closed corner in \(\cA_N\).

We shall use the following support consequence: if \(B,C,D\) are hereditary
subalgebras as above and
\[
        s_C(s_BA^{**}s_B)s_C\subset s_DA^{**}s_D,                \tag{2}
\]
then
\[
        q(C)\bigl(q(B)\cA_Nq(B)\bigr)q(C)\subset q(D)\cA_Nq(D).   \tag{3}
\]
This follows from (1).  We also use the elementary rectangular fact
\[
R^*R\in sA^{**}s,\quad S^*S\in tA^{**}t
       \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R^*S\in sA^{**}t,               \tag{4}
\]
because \(R=Rs\) and \(S=St\).

The next lemma supplies the coefficient-boundary identification used below.
For a von Neumann subalgebra \(B\subset N\), set
\[
        \cH_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]

\begin{lemma}[induced subalgebra boundary]\label{lem:coeff}
For every expected subalgebra \(B\subset N\),
\[
        X_{\cH_B}=X_B ,
\]
where the left hand side is DKEP's coefficient boundary of the \(N\)-\(N\)
correspondence \(\cH_B\), and \(X_B\) is the subalgebra boundary of DKEP
Example 3.4.  In the tracial case \(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\) as an
\(N\)-\(N\) correspondence.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and \(K={}_BL^2N_N\).  DKEP Example 5.11 gives
\(X_H=X_B\).  On the \(B\)-side, the coefficient boundary is all
\(\mathbb B(L^2B)\), since for the vector \(\widehat 1\) the coefficient
\(T_{\widehat1}T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}\) (for \(H\)) and
\(T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\widehat1}\) (for \(K\)) is \(1_{L^2B}\).  Hence
\(H\) is mixing relative to \(X_B\times\mathbb B(L^2B)\), and \(K\) is mixing
relative to \(\mathbb B(L^2B)\times\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  DKEP Theorem 5.10 and
the fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) show this explicitly:
if \(T_\xi\in\mathbb B(L^2N,L^2B)X_B\) and \(\eta\) is bounded in \(K\), then
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\in\mathbb B(L^2N)X_B\), and such elementary tensors are
cyclic.  Thus \(H\otimes_BK=\cH_B\) is mixing relative to
\(X_B\times\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  By the minimality assertion in DKEP Example 5.11,
\(X_{\cH_B}\subset X_B\).

Conversely, if \(\xi,\eta\) are bounded vectors in \(H\), then
\(\xi\otimes\widehat1\) and \(\eta\otimes\widehat1\) are bounded in
\(\cH_B\), and
\[
 T_{\xi\otimes\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\eta\otimes\widehat1}
 =T_\xi^{\,*}(T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\widehat1})T_\eta
 =T_\xi^{\,*}T_\eta .
\]
Thus every coefficient generator of \(X_H=X_B\) belongs to \(X_{\cH_B}\), so
\(X_B\subset X_{\cH_B}\).  Finally, the unitary
\(\overline{\xi\otimes\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes J\xi\) identifies
\(\overline{\cH_B}\) with \(\cH_B\).
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\subset V\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\); Lemma
\ref{lem:coeff} and heredity give \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\).

\begin{lemma}[direct sums and finite coefficient algebras]\label{lem:dsupport}
Let \(X\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) be a hereditary boundary piece with support
\(s_X\).  If an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(\cK=\oplus_{j\in J}\cK_j\) is a
direct sum and \(X_{\cK_j}\subset X\) for every \(j\), then every coefficient of
\(\cK\) lies in \(s_XA^{**}s_X\).

For \(U\subset V\), let \(\cF_U\) be the class of non-empty finite Connes
fusions of copies of
\(\cH_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) and \(\overline{\cH_U}\).  Define
\[
 \mathcal C_U=\operatorname{span}\{\,T_\xi^*T_\eta:
       \xi\in\cK,\ \eta\in\cL \hbox{ bounded},\ \cK,\cL\in\cF_U\,\};
\]
these are well-defined operators on \(L^2M\), although \(\xi,\eta\) may lie in
different \(M\)-\(M\) correspondences.  Then \(\mathcal C_U\) is a self-adjoint
algebra, and \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive approximate unit contained in the
norm closure of \(\mathcal C_U\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(p_F\) be the bimodular projection of \(\cK\) onto a finite subsum and put
\(\xi_F=p_F\xi\).  In DKEP's notation \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\), so bimodularity
gives uniformly bounded cutdowns and
\(T_{\xi_F}=L_\xi^*p_FR_\xi\to T_\xi\) strongly.  For finite \(F\), write
\(\xi_F=\sum_{j\in F}\xi_j\) and similarly for \(\eta_F\); then
\(T_{\xi_F}=\sum_{j\in F}T_{\xi_j}\).  The diagonal coefficients
\(T_{\xi_j}^*T_{\xi_j}\) and
\(T_{\eta_k}^*T_{\eta_k}\) are supported by \(s_X\); hence the rectangular
support relation (4) gives \(T_{\xi_j}^*T_{\eta_k}\in s_XA^{**}s_X\).  Thus
\(T_{\xi_F}^*T_{\eta_F}\in s_XA^{**}s_X\), and the strong convergence, with uniform bounds, gives
\(T_{\xi_F}^*T_{\eta_F}\to T_\xi^*T_\eta\) weak-* in \(A^{**}\).  Since the corner
\(s_XA^{**}s_X\) is weak-* closed, the first assertion follows.

For the second assertion, if
\(a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2}\) and \(b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}\) are elementary
rectangular coefficients from \(\cF_U\), then
\[
 ab=T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2},
\]
using \(T_{\overline{\xi_2}}=T_{\xi_2}^*\) and
\(T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha\).  The left vector again lies in a
finite \(U\)-fusion, so products remain in \(\mathcal C_U\); adjoints are
obvious.  By Lemma \ref{lem:coeff}, \(X_{M_U}=X_{\cH_U}\), and this hereditary
algebra is generated by the positive coefficients \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) of
\(\cH_U\) (off-diagonal coefficients follow by polarization).  The standard
net
\[
 f_\varepsilon\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^n T_{\xi_j}^*T_{\xi_j}\Bigr),
        \qquad f_\varepsilon(0)=0,
\]
over finite positive sums and \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a contractive
approximate unit for the hereditary algebra generated by these positives.  Each
term is a norm limit of polynomials with zero constant term in an element of
\(\mathcal C_U\), hence belongs to \(\overline{\mathcal C_U}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[boundary-corner intersection]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
        q_W(q_U\cA_Mq_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\cA_Mq_{U\cap W}.   \tag{5}
\]
Consequently, if \(U_1,\ldots,U_m\subset V\) and \(I=\cap_iU_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
\in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(s_U\) be the ordinary support of \(X_{M_U}\) in \(A_M^{**}\), and put
\(I=U\cap W\).  By (3), it suffices to prove
\[
        s_W(s_UA_M^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_IA_M^{**}s_I.             \tag{6}
\]

Choose, by Lemma \ref{lem:dsupport}, contractive approximate units
\(h_\alpha^W\in\overline{\mathcal C_W}\) and
\(h_\beta^U\in\overline{\mathcal C_U}\).  We claim
\[
        h_\alpha^Wh_\beta^U\in s_IA_M^{**}s_U,\qquad
        h_\beta^Uh_\alpha^W\in s_UA_M^{**}s_I .                  \tag{7}
\]
It is enough, by norm closure, to check this for elementary rectangular
coefficients.  Let \(a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2}\in\mathcal C_W\) and
\(b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}\in\mathcal C_U\), where the two \(\xi\)'s and the two
\(\eta\)'s may lie in different finite \(W\)- and \(U\)-fusions.  Then
\[
        ab=T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
                                                                        \tag{8}
\]
CDHJKN identify \(\cH_Y\) with \(H_Y(V,V)\), and their fusion rule
\cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN} gives
\(\cH_Y\otimes_M\cH_Z\) as a direct sum of copies of \(\cH_R\) with
\(R\subset Y\cap Z\).  Repeatedly applying this and using
\(\overline{\cH_Y}\cong\cH_Y\), any non-empty finite \(Y\)-fusion decomposes
over subsets of \(Y\), while a fusion containing both \(W\)- and \(U\)-blocks
decomposes over subsets of \(I=U\cap W\).  Hence the correspondence containing
\(\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1\) is a direct sum of \(\cH_Z\)'s
with \(Z\subset I\), and the one containing \(\eta_2\) is a direct sum of
\(\cH_Z\)'s with \(Z\subset U\).  Lemma \ref{lem:dsupport} and
\(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_I}\) (respectively \(X_{M_U}\)) show that the diagonal
coefficients of these two vectors are supported by \(s_I\) and \(s_U\).
The rectangular support fact (4) gives the first inclusion in (7), and the
second follows by taking adjoints.

For \(x\in s_UA_M^{**}s_U\), (7) implies
\[
h_\alpha^Wh_\beta^U\,x\,h_\gamma^Uh_\delta^W\in s_IA_M^{**}s_I .
\]
Since \(h_\alpha^W\to s_W\) and \(h_\beta^U\to s_U\) weak-* in \(A_M^{**}\),
successive weak-* limits yield (6).  Thus (5) follows from (3).  The
palindromic assertion follows by induction from (5).
\end{proof}

\section*{A finite boundary criterion}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be
boundary pieces and put \(q_i=q_{X_i}\).  Assume
\[
        q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0.  \tag{9}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to each \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume \(N\) is not properly proximal.  DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives an \(N\)-central
state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) with \(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\) and
write \(q=q_i\).  Since \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\),
\(q^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q^\perp\subset\widetilde S(N)\).  If
\(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), compression by \(q^\perp\) gives an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  For \(x\in N_+\), \(q\) commutes with \(x\), so
\[
0\le \omega(q^\perp xq^\perp)\le \omega(x)=\tau(x).
\]
Thus its restriction to \(N\) is normal; being a central positive functional on
the factor \(N\), it is a scalar multiple of \(\tau\), and after normalization
it restricts to \(\tau\).  This contradicts relative proper proximality.  Thus
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for every \(i\).

Extend \(\omega\) to a state on \(\cA_N\).  In the GNS representation every
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector, so the positive contraction in (9) has
\(\omega\)-value \(1\).  Since it lies in \(q_0\cA_Nq_0\), we get
\(\omega(q_0)=1\).  DKEP's canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\(\Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\cA_Nq_0\) sends \(1\) to \(q_0\) and is the
identity on \(N\) after compression.  Hence \(\omega\circ\Theta\) is a Connes
hypertrace on \(\mathbb B(L^2N)\), contradicting non-amenability of \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and suppose
\(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).  Assume that \(A\) contains a trace-zero unitary \(a\) and
that \(P\) contains unitaries \(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  In the AFP
decomposition of \(L^2N\), let \(p\) and \(q\) be the projections onto reduced
words whose first letter lies in \(H_P\) and \(H_Q\), respectively; then
\(1=e_B+p+q\).  For \(R_x=Jx^*J\), reduced-word inspection gives
\[
[p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\ [q,R_x]=0\quad(x\in P\ominus B),
\]
and the same formula with \(p,q\) interchanged for \(x\in Q\ominus B\); elements
of \(B\) commute with \(p,q\).  These commutators are \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-
translates of \(e_B\), so DKEP Lemma 6.1 implies \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) normal on \(N\).
Left multiplication by a centered \(P\)-letter sends \(qL^2N\) into \(pL^2N\),
and \(q w_1^*w_2q=0\) because \(E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\).  Hence the two ranges are
orthogonal and
\(w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p\).  Similarly, since \(a\in Q\ominus B\),
\(apa^*\le q\).  Centrality gives
\[
        2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q),
\]
so \(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).  The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every
non-zero power is a reduced alternating word and has \(B\)-expectation zero.
Thus the projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and have the same
\(\varphi\)-value; hence \(\varphi(e_B)=0\).  This contradicts
\(1=e_B+p+q\).  DKEP Theorem 6.2 gives proper proximality relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\) and let \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\).  The graph product splits as
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{10}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose trace-zero
unitaries \(u_r\in M_r,u_t\in M_t\) and put \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  If
\(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness and \(|V|\ge3\) give
\(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); put \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  In both
cases reduced-word calculus gives
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
The trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) is the unitary \(a\) in (10), so
Proposition \ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\in V\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
\(\cap_iL_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} gives
\[
 q_{L_{v_m}}\cdots q_{L_{v_1}}q_{L_{v_2}}\cdots q_{L_{v_m}}
 \in q_{\emptyset}\cA_Mq_{\emptyset}.
\]
Here \(M_\emptyset=\C\), and \(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 implies that \(M\) is a factor: the possible
obstructions would give either a universal vertex of \(\Gamma\) or an isolated
exceptional non-edge component, both impossible because \(\Gamma^c\) is
connected with at least three vertices.  CDHJKN Proposition 6.3 similarly
excludes amenability: its amenable non-edge components are single vertices or
isolated exceptional edges, whereas \(\Gamma^c\) is connected with at least
three vertices.  Thus \(M\) is a separable non-amenable finite factor.
Lemma \ref{lem:finite} applies and proves that \(M\) is properly proximal.
```

```latex
\section*{Problem interpretation}

The graph is finite, simple, and has at least three vertices.  The intended

partition $V=S\sqcup T$ such that every vertex of $S$ is adjacent to every
vertex of $T$.  Equivalently the complement graph $\Gamma^c$ is connected.
All vertex traces are faithful normal traces.  A trace-zero unitary in each
$M_v$ implies each vertex algebra is nontrivial and provides centered unitaries
for reduced-word and amalgamated-free-product arguments.

For $U\subset V$, $M_U$ denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
$E_U:M\to M_U$ the canonical expectation.  If $B\subset M$, $X_B$ is DKEP's
boundary piece generated by the Jones projection $e_B$.

\section*{DKEP normal-bidual notation}

Let
\[
  \mathcal A_N=(\mathbb B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*
\]
be DKEP's normal bidual.  For a boundary piece $X$, let $q_X$ be the support of
the normal corner $(\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*$ inside $\mathcal A_N$.  The
projection $q_X$ commutes with $N$ and with $JNJ$, and
\[
\widetilde S_X(N)=
\{T\in\mathcal A_N:[T,a]\in q_X\mathcal A_Nq_X
\text{ for every }a\in JNJ\}.

For $X=\mathcal K(L^2N)$ write $q_0$ and $\widetilde S(N)$.

DKEP Lemma 8.5 applies to the trivial group action when $N$ is a separable
finite factor: $N$ is properly proximal relative to $X$ iff there is no
$N$-central state on $\widetilde S_X(N)$ whose restriction to $N$ is the trace.

Important caution: a state on a von Neumann algebra which is $1$ on two
noncommuting projections need not be $1$ on their lattice meet, because the GNS
representation of a non-normal state need not preserve meets.  Thus the naive
criterion ``relative to finitely many $X_i$ plus
$\bigwedge_iq_{X_i}=q_0$'' is not justified unless commutation/normality is
available.  The replacement is to use an actual positive palindromic product of
the $q_i$'s which lies in the compact corner.

\section*{Palindromic finite-boundary criterion}

Let $N$ be a separable nonamenable finite factor and $X_1,\dots,X_m$ boundary
pieces.  Put $q_i=q_{X_i}$.  Suppose
\[
  R=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0.
\]
Then relative proper proximality with respect to every $X_i$ implies ordinary
proper proximality.

Proof details:
if $\omega$ is an $N$-central state on $\widetilde S(N)$ with $\omega|_N=\tau$,
then for each $i$,
\[
  q_i^\perp\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)q_i^\perp\subset \widetilde S(N)
\]
because $q_i$ commutes with $JNJ$ and $[T,JNJ]\subset q_i\mathcal A_Nq_i$.
If $\omega(q_i^\perp)>0$, compression by $q_i^\perp$ gives an $N$-central state
on $\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)$ normal on $N$ (normality is by domination by a scalar
multiple of $\tau$ on $N_+$), contradiction.  Hence the GNS cyclic vector for
$\omega$ is fixed by each $q_i$ and therefore by $R$.  Since $R$ is a positive
contraction in $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$, $1=\omega(R)\le \omega(q_0)$, so
$\omega(q_0)=1$.  Restricting $\omega$ to $q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\cong
\mathbb B(L^2N)$ gives a Connes hypertrace, contradicting nonamenability.

This implies by induction that the palindromic product associated with
$U_1,\dots,U_m$ lies in the corner for $\cap_iU_i$.

Reasoning.  Let $\mathcal H_U=L^2(M)\otimes_{M_U}L^2(M)$ with cyclic vector
$\zeta_U=1\otimes 1$.  The coefficient of $\zeta_U$ is $e_{M_U}$ and translates
of this vector generate DKEP's $X_{M_U}$.  DKEP's formula
$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ shows that products of a $W$-coefficient, a
$U$-coefficient, and a $W$-coefficient are coefficients of
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M \mathcal H_U\otimes_M \mathcal H_W
\]
(up to hereditary closure; the cyclic coefficient is
$e_{M_W}e_{M_U}e_{M_W}$).  Thus the normal corner on the left is contained in
the coefficient support of this triple fusion.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5 decompose this triple fusion as a direct sum of

X_{M_{U\cap W}}$, the whole coefficient boundary is contained in

This is intentionally weaker than the unsupported identity
$q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}$ and is tailored to non-normal states.

\section*{Relative Bass--Serre paradox}

For $N=P*_BQ$, $Q=B\bar\otimes A$, with $A$ containing a trace-zero unitary $a$
and with unitaries $w_1,w_2\in P$ satisfying
\[
  E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0,
\]
one proves $N$ properly proximal relative to $X_B$.

Let $p$ and $q$ be the projections onto the free-product Fock subspaces whose
first letter is in $L^2(P)\ominus L^2B$ and $L^2(Q)\ominus L^2B$, respectively.
Then $1=e_B+p+q$.

Commutator formulas with right multiplication $R_x=Jx^*J$:
for $x\in P\ominus B$,
\[
  [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [q,R_x]=0,
\]
and for $x\in Q\ominus B$,
\[
  [q,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\qquad [p,R_x]=0.
\]
Elements of $B$ commute with $p,q$.  These formulas are checked directly on
reduced words; only the $B$-summand and one-letter words from the same free
component can change first-letter type.  Since right translates of $e_B$ are in
the boundary ideal, DKEP Lemma 6.1 gives $p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)$.

If $\varphi$ is an $N$-central state on $S_{X_B}(N)$ normal on $N$, then
\[
  w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q.
\]
The first inequality uses centeredness of $w_i$ and orthogonality from
$E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0$; the second uses $a\in Q\ominus B$.  Centrality gives
$2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)$, so $\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0$.
The unitary $h=w_1a$ is $B$-Haar: the subspaces $h^nL^2B$ are pairwise
orthogonal.  Hence centrality forces $\varphi(e_B)=0$, contradicting
$1=e_B+p+q$.

\section*{Graph-product application}

For each vertex $v$,
\[
  M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{\Lk(v)}}(M_{\Lk(v)}\bar\otimes M_v).
\]
Let $C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim v\}$.  Complement connectedness gives
$C(v)\ne\emptyset$.  If $C(v)$ has two vertices $r,t$, use trace-zero unitaries
$u_r,u_t$ and set $w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t$.  If $C(v)=\{r\}$, complement
connectedness and $|V|\ge3$ give $s\in\Lk(v)$ with $r\not\sim s$; set
$w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r$.  In both cases expectations of $w_i$ and $w_1^*w_2$ onto
$M_{\Lk(v)}$ vanish.  The vertex unitary in $M_v$ gives $a$.  Therefore $M$ is
properly proximal relative to every $X_{M_{\Lk(v)}}$.

The intersection of all links is empty.  The palindromic product of the link
boundary supports is therefore in the compact corner because
$M_\emptyset=\mathbb C$ and $X_{M_\emptyset}$ is the compact boundary piece.

CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 gives factoriality.  In a connected complement graph
with at least three vertices, there is no universal vertex and no isolated
exceptional nonedge component.  CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.1 gives nonamenability:
if $M$ were amenable, applying the relative amenability criterion with
$V_1=V,V_2=\emptyset$ would force every complement edge to be an isolated
exceptional edge, impossible in a connected complement graph with at least
three vertices.

For a correspondence $\mathcal H$, write $X_{\mathcal H}$ for DKEP's
coefficient boundary generated by $T_\xi^*T_\eta$ for bounded vectors.
Let $q_{\mathcal H}$ be the support in the normal bidual of
$\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}(M)$.  A useful way to view this support is via the
normal left ideal $\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$ generated by the coefficient

closed hereditary corner generated by the coefficients.

$T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi$ gives the normal support-sandwich inclusion
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
 \subset
 q_{\mathcal H\otimes_M\mathcal K}\mathcal A

\]
Proof template: first verify on generators
\[
T_\xi^*(T_\eta^*T_{\eta'})T_{\xi'}

\]
Then use separate weak-* continuity and approximate the right support
$q_{\mathcal H}$ by an approximate unit for
$\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}^*\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}$.  This avoids assuming
commutation of supports or identifying their lattice meet.

Direct sums: the coefficient boundary of $\bigoplus_i\mathcal H_i$ is supported
by the join of the supports of the $X_{\mathcal H_i}$.  Diagonal coefficients
are immediate; off-diagonal coefficients are controlled by the usual
$2\times2$ positivity/Douglas factorization argument.  Hence if every summand's
boundary is contained in $X_Y$, then the whole direct-sum boundary is contained
in $X_Y$.

For graph products, with $\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M$, CDHJKN fusion

\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_U

\]
Since $Z\subset I$ implies $e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_I}$ and $X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_I}$,
the support of the fusion is dominated by $q_I$.  Combining with the
support-sandwich inclusion yields
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A q_U)q_W\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A q_{U\cap W}.
\]

In the finite-boundary criterion, do not identify
$q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0$ with $\mathbb B(L^2N)$.  DKEP's proof of Lemma 8.5 gives
the needed replacement: a natural $N$- and $JNJ$-bimodular u.c.p. map

  \Theta:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0

sending $1$ to $q_0$ and $x\in N$ to $q_0xq_0$.  If a central state
$\omega$ on $\widetilde S(N)$ satisfies $\omega(q_0)=1$, then
$\omega\circ\Theta$ is an $N$-central state on $\mathbb B(L^2N)$ extending the
trace.  This is a Connes hypertrace, forcing amenability of the finite factor.

The critic objected that the previous support-sandwich argument multiplied

\section*{Refined normal-left-ideal support mechanism}

For an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence \(\mathcal H\), DKEP's coefficient boundary
\(X_{\mathcal H}\) is generated hereditarily by the operators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\) bounded.  The most economical support object is
not the concrete \(C^*\)-algebra product
\(\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal K}}
\mathbb K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\), but the weak-* closed normal left ideal
\(\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\) generated by the coefficient maps \(T_\xi\).  Its
right support in \(\mathcal A_N=(B(L^2N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) is
\(q_{\mathcal H}\).  This follows because \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the hereditary
algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), and DKEP Proposition 5.9 identifies the
corresponding relative compact operators with the operators whose positive
squares lie in the relevant \(\mathbb K_X\)-corners.

Composition of rectangular normal bimodules is separately weak-* continuous in
the DKEP normal-bidual setting and agrees on bounded vectors with the concrete
Connes-fusion formula
\[
  T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi.
\]
Hence
\[
  \mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}
  \subset \mathcal L_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
Since \(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H}\in
\mathcal L_{\mathcal K}\mathcal L_{\mathcal H}\), for any
\(x=q_{\mathcal K}a q_{\mathcal K}\) one obtains
\[
 q_{\mathcal H}xq_{\mathcal H}
 =(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})^*a(q_{\mathcal K}q_{\mathcal H})

    q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the support-level sandwich needed for graph products and avoids the
unproved closed-\(\mathbb K_X\) product inclusion.

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), the induced correspondence
\(\mathcal H_B=L^2N\otimes_B L^2N\) has coefficient boundary \(X_B\).  The
cyclic vector \(\zeta=\hat1\otimes_B\hat1\) has coefficient operator \(e_B\),
and \(N\)-\(N\) translates of \(\zeta\) produce exactly the left/right
translates of \(e_B\) appearing in DKEP's definition of \(X_B\).

Direct sums of correspondences: if each summand \(\mathcal H_j\) has support
below a projection \(q\), then \(\oplus_j\mathcal H_j\) also has support below

corner fact \(a^*a,b^*b\in D\Rightarrow a^*b\in D\), proved from \(2\times2\)
positivity/Douglas factorization.  Infinite direct sums follow by truncating
bounded vectors.

\section*{Operator-system point in the palindromic criterion}

DKEP's \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) is an operator system, not generally a
\(C^*\)-algebra.  When a state \(\omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\) satisfies
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for several support projections \(q_i\), extend
\(\omega\) to a state \(\bar\omega\) on the ambient von Neumann algebra
\(\mathcal A_N\).  In the GNS representation of \(\bar\omega\), each
\(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector; hence the palindromic product
\((q_1\cdots q_m)^*(q_1\cdots q_m)\) has \(\omega\)-value one.  This justifies
the GNS step without pretending that \(\widetilde S(N)\) itself is a
\(C^*\)-algebra.

Compression normality: if \(q=q_X\) and \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), the compressed
functional \(T\mapsto \omega(q^\perp Tq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is normal on
\(N\) because \(q\) commutes with \(N\) and, on \(N_+\), it is dominated by
\(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\).



\section*{Ordinary-envelope support calculus after Round 8}

The normal-bidual map \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\to p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\)
should not be used multiplicatively.  The safe pattern is:
work in the ordinary enveloping algebra \(A^{**}\), prove a support inclusion
there, and only then multiply by \(p_{\rm nor}\), using DKEP Proposition 2.10
(commutation of \(p_{\rm nor}\) with supports of hereditary \(N,JNJ\)-bimodular
subalgebras).

For a hereditary subalgebra \(C\subset A\), write \(e_C\in A^{**}\) for its open
support.  If a concrete hereditary-product inclusion \(B C B\subset D\) is
available, then
\[
  e_B(e_C A^{**}e_C)e_B\subset e_D A^{**}e_D.
\]
Proof: approximate elements of \(e_C A^{**}e_C\) by bounded nets from \(C\),
sandwich by elements and then an approximate unit of \(B\), and pass to weak-*
limits.  Cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\) gives
\(q_B(q_C\mathcal A q_C)q_B\subset q_D\mathcal A q_D\).

For coefficient boundaries \(X_{\mathcal H}\), the desired support inclusion is
best viewed as a rectangular-support statement rather than as a product of
weak-* closed left ideals in the normal bidual.  In ordinary \(A^{**}\), the
support of \(X_{\mathcal H}\) is the right support of the rectangular module
generated by the concrete maps \(T_\xi\).  The concrete identities
\[
  T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*,\qquad T_{\alpha\otimes\beta}=T_\beta T_\alpha
\]
give
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 (T_{\xi_3}^*T_{\xi_4})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^*
 T_{\xi_4\otimes\bar\xi_3\otimes\eta_2}.
\]
DKEP Proposition 5.9 (polar decomposition for rectangular relative compact
operators) and Theorem 5.10 (mixing/relative compactness equivalences) are the
references for passing from coefficient generators to hereditary closures and
supports.  The resulting support estimate is
\[
  q_{\mathcal H}(q_{\mathcal K}\mathcal A_N q_{\mathcal K})q_{\mathcal H}
  \subset
  q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}
  \mathcal A_N
  q_{\mathcal H\otimes_N\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes_N\mathcal K}.
\]
This is the replacement for the invalid binary-fusion/normal-left-ideal
argument.

In the graph-product case
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) is self-contragredient and
\(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\).  CDHJKN Proposition 5.5 gives
\[
  \mathcal H_W\otimes_M\mathcal H_W
  \cong \bigoplus_{Z\subset W}\mathcal H_Z^{\oplus k_Z},
\]
and fusing each summand with \(\mathcal H_U\) gives only summands
\(\mathcal H_Y\) with \(Y\subset Z\cap U\subset W\cap U\).  Direct sums are
handled through DKEP Theorem 5.10: finite-support bounded vectors are cyclic for
the direct sum, so the support of a direct-sum correspondence is below the join
of the supports of its summands.  Hence
\[
  q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
  \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]

A point for further audit is the exact formulation of the DKEP rectangular
support statement: one should avoid claiming that \(\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}
\K_{X_{\mathcal K}}\K_{X_{\mathcal H}}\subset
\K_{X_{\mathcal H\otimes\bar{\mathcal H}\otimes\mathcal K}}\) for arbitrary
correspondences, since it is too strong when \(\mathcal H\) is the identity
correspondence.  The correct assertion is the support-corner inclusion above.



\section*{Refined graph-product support route}

A safer way to prove the graph-product boundary intersection is to avoid an
abstract support-sandwich theorem for all correspondences and work with the
concrete coefficient algebras for the graph-product basic correspondences.

First, for a DKEP boundary piece \(X\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\), let
\(e_X\in A^{**}\) be the ordinary open support.  The normal support used in
DKEP for \((\mathbb K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) agrees, after cutting by
\(p_{\rm nor}\), with the ordinary support of \(X\):
\[
        q_X=p_{\rm nor}e_X .
\]
Reason: \(X\subset \mathbb K_X(N)\) gives \(p_{\rm nor}e_X\le q_X\).  Conversely,
if a functional in \(A^{\sharp_J}\) vanishes on \(X\), DKEP's strong-closure
description of \(\mathbb K_X\) (the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)/strong
\(N,JNJ\)-bimodule closure in Section 3) forces it to vanish on
\(\mathbb K_X\).  Thus \(X\) and \(\mathbb K_X\) have the same annihilator among
normal-bimodular functionals, hence the same normal support.  This is the bridge
between the concrete coefficient boundary and the \(q_X\) appearing in
\(\widetilde S_X\).

For \(U\subset V\), set
\[
        \mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M .
\]
The vector \(\hat1\otimes_{M_U}\hat1\) has \(T_\zeta=P_U\), the Jones projection.
Its \(M\)-\(M\) translates generate \(X_{M_U}\) (DKEP Example 3.4/5.11).  Let
\(B_U\) be the norm-closed span of coefficient operators coming from non-empty finite
Connes fusions of the correspondences \(\mathcal H_Z\) and
\(\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\) with \(Z\subset U\).  The DKEP identities for
\(T\)-operators make this a \(C^*\)-algebra, and CDHJKN fusion decomposes all
such non-empty finite fusions into \(\mathcal H_L\)'s with \(L\subset U\).  Since
\(L\subset U\) implies \(X_{M_L}\subset X_{M_U}\) (because \(P_L\in P_U
\mathbb B(L^2M)P_U\)), this algebra has the same support as \(X_{M_U}\).  Let \(s_U\)
be this support in \(A^{**}\).  Then
\[
        q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}=p_{\rm nor}s_U .
\]

The elementary \(C^*\)-support lemma needed is:

If \(B,C\subset A\) are \(C^*\)-subalgebras with supports \(r,s\), and if a
projection \(p\in A^{**}\) satisfies
\[
        BC\subset pA^{**}s,\qquad CB\subset sA^{**}p,
\]
then
\[
        r(sA^{**}s)r\subset pA^{**}p.
\]
Proof: let approximate units of \(B\) and \(C\) converge weak-* to \(r\) and
\(s\).  For \(x\in sA^{**}s\), the elements \(b_\alpha c_\beta x
c_{\beta'}b_{\alpha'}\) lie in \(pA^{**}p\), and successive weak-* limits give
the claim.

For graph products, the needed product-support hypotheses are
\[
        B_WB_U\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_U,\qquad
        B_UB_W\subset s_UA^{**}s_{U\cap W}.
\]
On coefficient generators, the first inclusion follows from
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]
The \(\xi\)-vectors are from finite fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z\)'s with
\(Z\subset W\), and the \(\eta\)-vectors from finite fusions with \(Y\subset U\).
Since \(\mathcal H_Z\cong\overline{\mathcal H_Z}\), CDHJKN Proposition 5.5
decomposes the correspondence containing
\(\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1\) into copies of \(\mathcal H_L\) with
\(L\subset U\cap W\), while \(T_{\eta_2}\) has right support \(s_U\).  The
second inclusion is the adjoint.  Norm closure extends this from generators to
\(B_WB_U\) and \(B_UB_W\).

Combining these product-support inclusions with the ordinary support lemma gives
\[
        s_W(s_UA^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_{U\cap W}.
\]
Finally \(p_{\rm nor}\) commutes with the supports by DKEP Proposition 2.10, so
cutting gives
\[
        q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
        \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
This route avoids the false/too-strong assertion
\(\mathbb K_{X_H}\mathbb K_{X_K}\mathbb K_{X_H}\subset \mathbb K_{X_L}\) and
also avoids needing a general correspondence support theorem beyond the
concrete graph-product coefficient/fusion calculation.



\section*{Round 10 consolidated proof details}

For a boundary piece \(X\subset B(L^2N)\), the normal support used in DKEP
Lemma 8.5 should be treated as the support of DKEP's hereditary algebra
\(K_X(N)\), not naively as the support of the originally displayed generators.
DKEP Prop. 2.10 says: if \(B\subset A=B(L^2N)\) is a \(C^*\)-algebra which is
simultaneously an \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule with faithful multiplier actions,
and \(s_B\in A^{**}\) is its ordinary support, then
\[
  (B^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}s_BA^{**}s_Bp_{\rm nor}
\]
inside \((A^{\sharp_J})^*=p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\), and \(s_B\) commutes
with \(p_{\rm nor}\).  For \(B=K_X(N)\) this gives \(q_X=p_{\rm nor}s_B\).

For the graph-product subalgebra \(M_U\), \(K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\) can be described
as the norm-closed coefficient algebra generated by nonempty finite Connes
fusions of \(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\) and conjugates with
\(Z\subset U\).  The coefficient formulas
\[
  (T_\xi^*T_\eta)(T_\alpha^*T_\beta)
    =T_{\xi\otimes\bar\eta\otimes\alpha}^{\,*}T_\beta,\qquad
  (T_\xi^*T_\eta)^*=T_\eta^*T_\xi
\]
make this a \(C^*\)-algebra.  CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 imply that
finite fusions over subsets of \(U\) decompose into direct sums of
\(\mathcal H_L\)'s with \(L\subset U\), and fusing an \(U\)-piece with a
\(W\)-piece only produces \(L\subset U\cap W\).

Rectangular support principle used in the intersection lemma: if \(D\subset A\)
has open support \(d\), \(T:L^2N\to H\) is bounded, and \(T^*T\in D\), then for
an approximate unit \(u_i\) of \(D\), \(Tu_i\to T\) strongly; hence \(T=Td\) in
the bidual sense.  Therefore if \(S^*S\in C\) and \(C\) has support \(c\), then
\(T^*S\in dA^{**}c\).  This closes the previous rectangular gap in the product
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]

Relative Bass--Serre paradox for \(N=P*_BQ\), \(Q=B\bar\otimes A\): use the
AFP decomposition
\[
L^2N=L^2B\oplus\bigoplus H_{i_1}^\circ\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n}^\circ,
\qquad i_j\in\{P,Q\},\ i_j\ne i_{j+1}.
\]
Let \(p,q\) be the projections onto words with first letter \(P,Q\).  For
\(R_x=Jx^*J\),
\[
 [p,R_x]=R_xe_B-e_BR_x,\ [q,R_x]=0\quad (x\in P\ominus B),
\]
and with \(p,q\) interchanged for \(x\in Q\ominus B\).  These commutators are in
\(X_B\), so DKEP Lemma 6.1 yields \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).  If \(w_1,w_2\in P\)
satisfy \(E_B(w_i)=E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0\) and \(a\in A\subset Q\) is a trace-zero
unitary, then \(w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p\), \(apa^*\le q\), and
\(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for \(n\ne0\).  This forces any
\(N\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) normal on \(N\) to vanish on
\(p,q,e_B\), contradiction.

Graph combinatorics preserved: connected \(\Gamma^c\), \(|V|\ge3\), implies
each vertex has a complement-neighbor.  If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), then \(r\) has a
complement-neighbor \(s\ne v\); necessarily \(s\in\Lk(v)\).  Thus the
Bass--Serre unitaries can be chosen as \(u_r,u_t\) or \(u_r,u_su_r\).
CDHJKN factoriality and amenability criteria exclude factor failure and
amenability because a universal vertex or an exceptional nonedge pair would be
an isolated vertex/edge of \(\Gamma^c\).



\section*{Round 11 refinement: normal support and coefficient model}

To avoid the earlier unsupported identity
\[
  K_{X_{M_U}}(M)=\text{``finite fusion coefficient algebra''},
\]
it is better to compute the normal-bidual support \(q_{X_{M_U}}\) from the
original DKEP boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\).  The relevant facts are:

\begin{itemize}
\item If \(B\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra
which is an \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodule with faithful multiplier actions, and
\(s_B\in A^{**}\) is its ordinary open support, then the unit of
\((B^{\sharp_J})^*\subset (A^{\sharp_J})^*\) is \(p_{\rm nor}s_B\).  This is
not quoted as a literal statement of DKEP Proposition 2.10; rather it follows
from the paragraph before DKEP Remark 2.8 (one normal category) plus Proposition
2.10 for the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal category.  Equivalently, an
approximate unit of \(B\) converges to \(p_{\rm nor}s_B\) in the normal bidual.
\item DKEP Proposition 3.6 gives
\(K_X^{\infty,1}=\overline{X}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}
=\overline{K_X}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}\).  Since the \(\sharp_J\)-functionals
are continuous for this closure, \(X\) and \(K_X\) have the same annihilator in
\(A^{\sharp_J}\).  Thus \(q_X\) may be computed as the normal support of the
original boundary piece \(X\).
\item For \(U\subset V\), the boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\) is the amplified
coefficient algebra of the \(M\)-\(M\) correspondence
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).  The cyclic vector \(1\otimes1\) has
coefficient \(e_{M_U}\), and Hilbert amplifications account for the arbitrary
corner \(e_{M_U}\mathbb B(L^2M)e_{M_U}\) appearing in DKEP Example 3.4.
\item Infinite direct sums should be handled by support, not by norm
approximation of bounded vectors.  If \(\mathcal H=\oplus_i\mathcal H_i\) and
the coefficients of all \(\mathcal H_i\)'s are supported by \(s\), then every
coefficient of a bounded vector in \(\mathcal H\) is in \(sA^{**}s\): finite
coordinate truncations converge strong-* at the \(T_\xi\)-operator level, so
their coefficients converge weak-*.
\item With \(s_U=\operatorname{supp}(X_{M_U})\), the key product is
\(X_{M_W}X_{M_U}\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_U\).  For coefficients
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =
 T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]
The left vector belongs to an amplification of
\(\mathcal H_W\otimes\overline{\mathcal H_W}\otimes\mathcal H_U\).  Since
\(\overline{\mathcal H_W}\cong\mathcal H_W\), CDHJKN Theorem 5.4 and
Proposition 5.5 decompose this as a direct sum of \(\mathcal H_Z\)'s with
\(Z\subset U\cap W\).  The rectangular support lemma then gives the required
product inclusion.  Applying approximate units yields
\(s_W(s_UA^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_{U\cap W}\), and cutting by
\(p_{\rm nor}\) gives the normal-bidual corner intersection.
\end{itemize}

This support-based approach directly addresses the earlier criticism about
finite-support vectors in infinite direct sums: the proof only uses rectangular support for finite off-diagonal component coefficients, strong-*
convergence of coordinate truncations, and weak-* closedness of support corners,
not operator-norm convergence of \(T_{\xi_F}\) to \(T_\xi\).


\section*{Round 12 refined support proof}

The current answer uses a more robust version of the graph-product boundary
intersection, avoiding the earlier unsupported density assertion
``\(X_{M_U}\) is norm-spanned by amplified coefficients''.

Normal-bidual support.  Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and let \(p_{\rm nor}\) be
the projection implementing the simultaneous \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-normal bidual
\((A^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For a hereditary \(N,JNJ\)-bimodular subalgebra
\(B\subset A\), the normal-bidual support of \(B\) is obtained from the ordinary
open support \(s_B\in A^{**}\):
\[
        q(B)=p_{\rm nor}s_B=s_Bp_{\rm nor}.
\]
The justification is the one-normal cutdown in the paragraph before DKEP
Remark 2.8 plus DKEP Proposition 2.10 for the simultaneous normal category.  A
contractive approximate unit of \(B\) converges ultraweakly to \(q(B)\) in the
normal bidual.  For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\), DKEP Proposition 3.6 and
Proposition 2.6 imply that \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have identical annihilators in
\(A^{\sharp_J}\), so \(q_X\) may be computed from the original boundary piece.

Coefficient model for \(X_{M_U}\).  For
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\), the vector
\(\Omega_U=1\otimes 1\) has \(T_{\Omega_U}=e_{M_U}\).  DKEP Example 5.11
therefore gives a coefficient boundary \(X_{\mathcal H_U}\) containing
\(e_{M_U}\); because it is hereditary and \(M,JMJ\)-invariant, it contains the
hereditary algebra generated by the translated \(e_{M_U}\)'s, i.e. DKEP
Example 3.4's \(X_{M_U}\).  Conversely, coefficients of \(M\)-\(M\) translates
of \(\Omega_U\) are translated \(e_{M_U}\)-coefficients, so
\(X_{\mathcal H_U}\subset X_{M_U}\).  Hence \(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\), and
heredity explains why the full corner \(e_{M_U}A e_{M_U}\) is present.  If
\(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\), hence \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\)
and \(s_Z\le s_U\).

Special approximate units.  Although the whole hereditary algebra \(X_{M_U}\)
need not be norm-spanned by simple coefficients, it has a contractive
approximate unit contained in the \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the positive
coefficient generators.  Taking functions \(f_\varepsilon(\sum T_{\xi_j}^*
T_{\xi_j})\), with \(f_\varepsilon(0)=0\), produces such an approximate unit.
Polynomials in the finite sums expand into monomials in coefficient generators,
and DKEP's formulas
\[
        T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*,\qquad
        T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi
\]
rewrite those monomials as finite sums of coefficients of finite Connes fusions
of \(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\).  Thus we can choose
approximate units \(h_\alpha^U\) which are norm limits of finite-fusion
coefficients.

Fusion support.  CDHJKN Proposition 5.5, applied repeatedly, says that any
finite fusion whose labels all lie in \(U\) decomposes as a direct sum of
\(\mathcal H_Z=L^2M\otimes_{M_Z}L^2M\)'s with \(Z\subset U\).  If the labels of
one block lie in \(W\) and the next block lies in \(U\), then after reducing the
\(W\)-block and fusing with the \(U\)-block only labels \(Z\subset W\cap U\)
remain.  Direct sums are handled at the support level: coordinate truncations of
bounded vectors preserve boundedness and the associated \(T\)-operators
converge strong-*, so coefficients are weak-* limits of finite-coordinate
coefficients.  Off-diagonal finite-coordinate coefficients are controlled by
the rectangular support fact \(T^*T\in dA^{**}d,\ S^*S\in cA^{**}c
\Rightarrow T^*S\in dA^{**}c\).

Boundary intersection mechanism.  Let \(I=U\cap W\).  For the special
approximate units just described,
\[
        h_\alpha^W h_\beta^U\in s_I A^{**}s_U,\qquad
        h_\beta^U h_\alpha^W\in s_U A^{**}s_I.
\]
On finite-fusion coefficient terms this follows from
\[
 (T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2})(T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2})
 =T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]
The left vector is supported by \(s_I\) by the preceding fusion calculation,
and the right vector by \(s_U\); the rectangular support lemma gives the
rectangular containment.  Then for \(x\in s_UA^{**}s_U\),
\[
s_Wxs_W=\text{weak-*}\lim_{\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta}
 h_\alpha^W h_\beta^U x h_\gamma^U h_\delta^W\in s_IA^{**}s_I.
\]
Cutting by \(p_{\rm nor}\) gives
\[
        q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
        \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]
This is the central repair: one only needs special approximate units in
\(X_{M_U}\), not norm-density of all of \(X_{M_U}\) by simple coefficients.



\section*{Refined normal-support calculus using DKEP Theorem 5.6}

For \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\(\mathcal A_N=(A^{\sharp_J})^*\), avoid representing \(\mathcal A_N\) as
\(p_{\rm nor}A^{**}p_{\rm nor}\) with a central projection.  The safe statement
is the following.

If \(B\subset A\) is a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra invariant under the
multiplier actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), and \(s_B\in A^{**}\) is the ordinary
open support, then DKEP's one-normal support paragraph, applied separately to
the two normal structures, together with Proposition 2.10 gives
\[
        (B^{\sharp_J})^*=\mathcal A_N\cap s_BA^{**}s_B
\]
inside \(A^{**}\).  This is a corner of \(\mathcal A_N\); its unit is denoted
\(q(B)\).  Thus a support inclusion in the ordinary enveloping algebra passes
to the normal bidual: if
\[
        s_C(s_BA^{**}s_B)s_C\subset s_DA^{**}s_D,
\]
then
\[
        q(C)(q(B)\mathcal A_Nq(B))q(C)\subset q(D)\mathcal A_Nq(D).
\]
This avoids any multiplicativity assertion for the canonical map
\(A\to\mathcal A_N\).

For a DKEP boundary piece \(X\), the support \(q_X\) defined from
\(K_X(N)\) agrees with \(q(X)\), the normal support of the original hereditary
boundary piece.  The proof uses DKEP Theorem 5.6 rather than Proposition 2.6:
with \(X=Y\), condition (4) says that if
\(T\in K_{X,X}^{\infty,1}(N,N)=K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), then every
\(\sharp_J\)-functional vanishing on \(XB(L^2N)X\) vanishes on \(T\).  Since
\(X\) is hereditary, \(XB(L^2N)X\) is norm dense in \(X\).  Hence any
\(\sharp_J\)-functional vanishing on \(X\) vanishes on \(K_X(N)\), because
\(K_X(N)\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\).  The converse is \(X\subset K_X(N)\).
Thus \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) have the same annihilator in \(A^{\sharp_J}\), hence
the same weak-* closed corner in \(\mathcal A_N\).

\section*{Graph-product boundary intersection in ordinary supports}

For \(U\subset V\), let \(s_U\) be the ordinary open support of the concrete
boundary piece \(X_{M_U}\).  The coefficient correspondence
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) has cyclic vector
\(\Omega_U=1\otimes1\) with \(T_{\Omega_U}=e_{M_U}\); hence
\(X_{\mathcal H_U}=X_{M_U}\).  If \(Z\subset U\), then
\(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\), and \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\).

A useful approximate unit for \(X_{M_U}\) is obtained from finite sums of
positive coefficient generators:
\[
        f_\varepsilon\left(\sum_{j=1}^n T_{\xi_j}^*T_{\xi_j}\right),
        \qquad f_\varepsilon(0)=0 .
\]
Polynomial approximation and the identities \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\) and
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) show that these approximate-unit elements
are norm limits of the rectangular finite-fusion coefficient algebra generated by
\(\mathcal H_U\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\).

For rectangular finite-fusion coefficients
\(a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2}\) from \(W\)-labels and
\(b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}\) from \(U\)-labels,
\[
        ab=T_{\xi_1\otimes\bar\xi_2\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2}.
\]
CDHJKN's fusion rule for \(H_U(V,V)=\mathcal H_U\) implies that the left vector
is supported by a direct sum of \(\mathcal H_Z\)'s with
\(Z\subset U\cap W\), while the right vector is supported by \(s_U\).  The
rectangular fact \(T^*T\in sA^{**}s,\ S^*S\in tA^{**}t\Rightarrow
T^*S\in sA^{**}t\) gives
\[
        X_{M_W}X_{M_U}\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_U
\]
at the level of the chosen approximate-unit nets, and similarly for the
adjoint product.  Passing to weak-* limits of approximate units gives
\[
        s_W(s_UA^{**}s_U)s_W\subset s_{U\cap W}A^{**}s_{U\cap W}.
\]
The normal-support passage above then gives
\[
        q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
        \subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}.
\]



\section*{Coefficient boundary of the induced subalgebra correspondence}

Let \(B\subset N\) be an expected inclusion of finite tracial von Neumann
algebras.  Write
\[
H={}_N L^2N_B,\qquad K={}_B L^2N_N,\qquad
\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_B K.
\]
DKEP Example 5.11 says \(X_H=X_B\), where \(X_B\) is the boundary piece of
Example 3.4.  On the \(B\)-side of \(H\), the vector \(\widehat 1\) gives
\(T_{\widehat 1}T_{\widehat 1}^{*}=1_{L^2B}\), so the right coefficient
boundary \(Y_H\) is all of \(\mathbb B(L^2B)\).  Likewise, for \(K\), the left
coefficient boundary is all of \(\mathbb B(L^2B)\).  Hence \(H\) is mixing
relative to \(X_B\times\mathbb B(L^2B)\), and \(K\) is mixing relative to
\(\mathbb B(L^2B)\times\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  Using DKEP Theorem 5.10 in condition
(1), the cyclic set of bounded elementary tensors in \(H\otimes_BK\), and
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,
\]
one gets that \(\mathcal H_B\) is mixing relative to
\(X_B\times\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  By the minimality property of Example 5.11,
\(X_{\mathcal H_B}\subset X_B\).

The opposite inclusion is even more direct.  If \(\xi,\eta\) are bounded
vectors in \(H={}_NL^2N_B\), then \(\xi\otimes\widehat1\) and
\(\eta\otimes\widehat1\) are bounded in \(\mathcal H_B\), and
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes\widehat1}^*T_{\eta\otimes\widehat1}
        =T_\xi^*(T_{\widehat1}^*T_{\widehat1})T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta,
\]
because \(T_{\widehat1}^*T_{\widehat1}=1_{L^2B}\) for
\(K={}_BL^2N_N\).  Thus all coefficient generators of \(X_H=X_B\) are
coefficient generators for \(X_{\mathcal H_B}\), and
\(X_B\subset X_{\mathcal H_B}\).  Hence
\[
        X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B.
\]
In the tracial setting the conjugate of \(\mathcal H_B\) is isomorphic to
\(\mathcal H_B\) through
\[
        \overline{\xi\otimes\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes J\xi .
\]

For \(Z\subset U\), \(e_{M_Z}\leq e_{M_U}\); since \(e_{M_U}\in X_{M_U}\) and
\(X_{M_U}\) is hereditary, \(e_{M_Z}\in X_{M_U}\), and multiplier invariance then
gives \(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\).

\section*{Direct-sum and support bookkeeping for coefficient operators}

If \(X\subset A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) is hereditary with open support \(s_X\), the
associated left ideal is \(L_X=\{R\in A:R^*R\in X\}\), and
\(L_X^* A L_X\subset X\).  In support language,
\[
        R^*R\in sA^{**}s,\quad S^*S\in tA^{**}t
        \quad\Rightarrow\quad R^*S\in sA^{**}t,
\]
because \(R=Rs\) and \(S=St\).

If a correspondence \(\mathcal K=\oplus_j \mathcal K_j\) is a direct sum and
\(X_{\mathcal K_j}\subset X\) for all \(j\), then coefficients of \(\mathcal K\)
are supported by \(s_X\).  For finite sums this follows from the left-ideal
argument: each component coefficient has diagonal support in \(X\), and
off-diagonal terms are rectangular products.  Infinite sums are obtained by
cutting bounded vectors with finite coordinate projections \(p_F\).  In DKEP's
notation \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\), hence \(T_{p_F\xi}=L_\xi^*p_FR_\xi\) converges
strongly to \(T_\xi\) with uniform bounds; the coefficient products then tend
weak-* to \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) in \(A^{**}\).

For graph-product applications the useful algebra is the rectangular
finite-fusion coefficient algebra.  Let \(\mathcal F_U\) be the class of
non-empty finite Connes fusions of
\(\mathcal H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) and \(\overline{\mathcal H_U}\).  Define
\[
 \mathcal C_U=\operatorname{span}\{T_\xi^*T_\eta:
    \xi\in\mathcal K,\ \eta\in\mathcal L \text{ bounded},\
    \mathcal K,\mathcal L\in\mathcal F_U\}.
\]
Since all \(\mathcal K,\mathcal L\) are \(M\)-\(M\) correspondences, the
operators \(T_\xi,T_\eta\) act on \(L^2M\); \(\xi\) and \(\eta\) do not have to
lie in the same correspondence.  If
\(a=T_{\xi_1}^*T_{\xi_2}\) and \(b=T_{\eta_1}^*T_{\eta_2}\), then
\[
        ab
        =T_{\xi_1\otimes\overline{\xi_2}\otimes\eta_1}^{\,*}T_{\eta_2},
\]
where the left vector lies in a finite \(U\)-fusion.  Thus \(\mathcal C_U\) is a
self-adjoint algebra.  Since \(X_{M_U}=X_{\mathcal H_U}\), it contains the
positive coefficient generators of \(X_{M_U}\), and a contractive approximate
unit for \(X_{M_U}\) is obtained from
\[
 f_\varepsilon\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^n T_{\xi_j}^*T_{\xi_j}\Bigr),\qquad
 f_\varepsilon(0)=0,
\]
with finite sums of positive \(\mathcal H_U\)-coefficients.  Polynomial
approximation by polynomials vanishing at zero places these approximate-unit
elements in \(\overline{\mathcal C_U}\).
```

## Referee report

### Overall verdict

`answer.tex` **compiles and satisfies the stated LaTeX-format contract**, but it is **not mathematically ready**. The proof’s central “boundary-corner intersection” mechanism rests on a direct-sum support lemma whose proof contains a serious topological error: it treats strong-operator convergence in the standard representation as weak-* convergence in the universal enveloping bidual \(A^{**}\). That implication is false. Since this lemma is essential for placing the palindromic product of boundary support projections in the compact corner, the final application of the finite boundary criterion is not justified.

I therefore cannot certify the solution as complete.

## Literature cross-check

The cited DKEP paper indeed defines the subalgebra boundary \(X_B\) in Example 3.4 as the norm-closed span generated from \(e_B B(L^2M)e_B\), and states that it is hereditary. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Example 5.11 defines coefficient boundary pieces \(X_H,Y_H\) and says they are the smallest boundary pieces for which a correspondence is mixing relative to them. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Theorem 5.10 includes the Connes-fusion identity \(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) DKEP Lemma 8.5 is correctly cited for the normal-bidual formulation, but it assumes ergodicity on the center; for the trivial action this is why the factor condition matters. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

The cited CDHJKN paper does contain the graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rules used in spirit by the answer: Main Theorem 0.4 / Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 decompose \(M_{V_1}L^2(M)_{M_{V_2}}\) and give fusion summands indexed by subsets of intersections. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) Its Main Theorem 0.5 gives the stated factoriality obstruction conditions under state-zero unitaries, and Proposition 6.3 gives the amenability criterion. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

So the references are broadly relevant, but several uses in `answer.tex` are not justified at the required level.

## Detailed audit

### 1. Problem interpretation

The answer explicitly adopts the standard interpretation that “irreducible” means not a nontrivial graph join, equivalently \(\Gamma^c\) connected. This is a reasonable interpretation of the problem statement, which is otherwise slightly ambiguous about whether the two subgraphs are required to be nonempty and disjoint.

No major issue here.

---

### 2. Normal support calculus

The formula

\[
(D^{\sharp_J})^*=\mathcal A_N\cap s_DA^{**}s_D
\tag{1}
\]

is plausible for hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebras with faithful multiplier actions of both \(N\) and \(JNJ\), but the answer states it rather generally. DKEP’s support discussion before Remark 2.8 requires normal \(M\)-\(C^*\)-algebra hypotheses and faithful multiplier action; the simultaneous-normal version is then obtained through Proposition 2.10. The answer should explicitly verify these hypotheses for every hereditary algebra to which it applies. This is not just cosmetic, because general DKEP boundary pieces need not have all of \(N\) and \(JNJ\) in their multiplier algebras; DKEP later replaces \(X\) by \(K_X(M)\) precisely to get better multiplier behavior.

The claim \(q_X=q(X)\) for an arbitrary DKEP boundary piece is also overgeneralized. For the specific subalgebra boundary pieces \(X_{M_U}\), this may be repairable, because Example 3.4 gives good multiplier invariance. But as written, the statement is not fully justified for arbitrary boundary pieces.

This issue is serious but probably repairable. It is not the main fatal flaw.

---

### 3. Lemma “induced subalgebra boundary”

The statement \(X_{\mathcal H_B}=X_B\) is plausible and probably true, but the proof has clarity and convention problems.

In particular, the sentence

> “for the vector \(\widehat1\) the coefficient \(T_{\widehat1}T_{\widehat1}^{*}\) (for \(H\)) and \(T_{\widehat1}^{*}T_{\widehat1}\) (for \(K\)) is \(1_{L^2B}\)”

is at least ambiguous and appears reversed under the convention later stated in the paper, \(T_\xi=L_\xi^*R_\xi\). With that convention, for \(H={}_NL^2N_B\), \(T_{\widehat1}:L^2B\to L^2N\) is the inclusion, so \(T_{\widehat1}^*T_{\widehat1}=1_{L^2B}\), while \(T_{\widehat1}T_{\widehat1}^*=e_B\). The analogous identity for \(K={}_BL^2N_N\) is reversed. This may only be a notational slip, but in a proof built around coefficient operators, the direction matters.

The minimality argument using DKEP Example 5.11 also needs more detail. Example 5.11 gives minimal boundary pieces for a given correspondence, but the answer only sketches why \(\mathcal H_B=H\otimes_BK\) is mixing relative to \(X_B\times B(L^2N)\). A direct computation from the cyclic vector \(1\otimes_B1\), whose coefficient is \(e_B\), would be cleaner.

This is a gap/ambiguity, but not by itself fatal if corrected.

---

### 4. Lemma “direct sums and finite coefficient algebras” — fatal flaw

This is the main mathematical failure.

The first assertion attempts to prove that if each summand \(\mathcal K_j\) has coefficient boundary supported by \(s_X\), then every coefficient of \(\bigoplus_j\mathcal K_j\) lies in \(s_XA^{**}s_X\). The proof uses finite-coordinate projections \(p_F\) and states:

> “the strong convergence, with uniform bounds, gives  
> \(T_{\xi_F}^*T_{\eta_F}\to T_\xi^*T_\eta\) weak-* in \(A^{**}\).”

This implication is false.

Here \(A=B(L^2N)\) is being treated as a \(C^*\)-algebra and \(A^{**}\) is its universal enveloping von Neumann algebra. Strong-operator convergence in the standard representation does **not** imply weak-* convergence in \(A^{**}\), i.e. convergence against all functionals in \(A^*\). It only gives convergence against normal functionals of the standard representation.

A concrete standard counterexample: in \(B(\ell^2\mathbb N)\), let \(P_n\) be the projection onto \(\overline{\mathrm{span}}\{e_k:k\ge n\}\). Then \(P_n\to0\) strongly, but a singular state obtained as an ultralimit of diagonal vector states satisfies \(\varphi(P_n)=1\) for all \(n\). Thus \(P_n\) does not converge to \(0\) in the \(\sigma(B(\ell^2),B(\ell^2)^*)\) topology, hence not weak-* in \(B(\ell^2)^{**}\).

This invalidates the proof that infinite direct-sum coefficients are supported by \(s_X\). Since CDHJKN fusion decompositions may have infinite multiplicity, this direct-sum support step is essential. The answer does not provide an alternative argument using DKEP’s \(\sharp_J\)-normal topology, Magajna strong-module closure, or a valid annihilator argument.

Therefore Lemma `\ref{lem:dsupport}` is not proved.

The second part of the lemma, constructing approximate units from finite-fusion coefficients, is more plausible, but it depends on the preceding coefficient-boundary identification and does not fix the failed support argument.

---

### 5. Boundary-corner intersection lemma

The boundary-corner intersection

\[
q_W(q_U\mathcal A_Mq_U)q_W
\subset q_{U\cap W}\mathcal A_Mq_{U\cap W}
\tag{5}
\]

is the heart of the proof. Its proof depends directly on the flawed direct-sum support lemma.

The use of CDHJKN fusion rules is conceptually correct: fusing \(H_W\)-blocks with \(H_U\)-blocks should produce summands indexed by subsets of \(W\cap U\). CDHJKN’s results support this kind of statement. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743)) But after obtaining a direct sum of correspondences, the answer again needs a valid argument that coefficients of arbitrary bounded vectors in the direct sum are supported by the appropriate boundary support. That is precisely the part left unproved because of the invalid weak-* convergence step above.

Consequently, the proof of (5) is incomplete. Since the palindromic compact-corner inclusion depends on (5), the later finite-boundary criterion cannot be applied.

This is a fatal gap.

---

### 6. Finite boundary criterion

The finite boundary criterion is largely plausible. The compression argument by \(q_i^\perp\) is reasonable because DKEP states that the support projection of \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\) commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) The GNS argument for the palindromic positive contraction is also plausible.

However, one step should be cited or expanded:

> “DKEP's canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map  
> \(\Theta:B(L^2N)\to q_0\mathcal A_Nq_0\) …”

DKEP’s proof of Lemma 8.5 indeed discusses natural \(M\)-bimodular u.c.p. maps into \((K_X(M)^{\sharp_J})^*\), but the answer should explicitly connect that construction to the compact case \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) and verify that \(\omega\circ\Theta\) is a Connes hypertrace. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517)) This is a missing detail, but likely repairable.

The finite criterion is not the main obstruction.

---

### 7. Relative Bass–Serre paradox

This proposition is plausible but terse.

The commutator formulas for the first-letter projections \(p,q\) are asserted by “reduced-word inspection.” This is acceptable as a sketch, but for a standalone proof the author should verify the cases where right multiplication by a centered letter cancels the final letter of a reduced word and may shorten the word to the \(B\)-summand. The projection inequalities

\[
w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p,\qquad apa^*\le q
\]

are also plausible, and the \(B\)-Haar argument for \(h=w_1a\) is standard. But again, the proof is compressed.

I would not reject solely on this section, but it would need expansion in a rigorous final proof.

---

### 8. Graph-product application

The graph-product splitting

\[
M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
(M_{L_v}\bar\otimes M_v)
\]

is standard. The combinatorial selection of \(w_1,w_2\) from non-neighbors of \(v\) is correct; I also brute-force checked the relevant finite-graph combinatorial implications for small vertex numbers. The final use of CDHJKN’s factoriality and amenability criteria is also essentially correct, provided one notes explicitly that in the tracial setting a trace-zero unitary lies in the centralizer and implies \(\dim M_v\ge2\). CDHJKN Main Theorem 0.5 and Proposition 6.3 support these uses. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

But this final step relies on the unproved boundary-corner intersection lemma, so the proof as a whole does not go through.

---

## Final assessment

The document is well organized and many components are plausible, but the proof is not complete. The fatal issue is the unsupported passage from strong convergence of finite-coordinate cutdowns to weak-* convergence in \(A^{**}\), which invalidates the direct-sum support lemma and therefore the boundary-corner intersection lemma. Since that intersection lemma is indispensable for producing the compact-corner palindromic product, the final conclusion is not rigorously established.

```latex
\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I use the standard graph-product meaning of irreducible: the finite graph
\(\Gamma=(V,E)\) is not a non-trivial join, equivalently the complement
\(\Gamma^c\) is connected.  The traces are faithful normal traces.  For
\(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and

\(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(N)\) restricting to the trace.

\section*{Normal supports and graph-product boundary corners}

Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  If \(X\subset A\) is a DKEP boundary piece with
\(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\), let \(q_X\) be the unit of the weak-* closure of \(X\)
in \(\cA_N\).  This is the same projection as the support of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\) used in DKEP Lemma 8.5.  Indeed \(X\subset K_X(N)\),
and DKEP Theorem 5.6 implies that every functional in \(A^{\sharp_J}\) which
vanishes on \(X\) vanishes on \(K_X(N)\): since \(X\) is hereditary,
\(XAX\subset X\), so Theorem 5.6(4) applies to all elements of \(K_X(N)\subset

\(A^{\sharp_J}\)-annihilator.  Consequently, for \(T\in A\),
\[
 \phi(T)=0\hbox{ for all }\phi\in A^{\sharp_J}\hbox{ with }\phi|_X=0
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T\in q_X\cA_Nq_X .                 \tag{1}
\]
In the von Neumann algebra \(\cA_N\) we shall use the elementary fact
\[
R^*R\in r\cA_Nr,\quad S^*S\in s\cA_Ns

\]
for projections \(r,s\in\cA_N\) and \(R,S\in A\).  Indeed
\(R(1-r)=0\) and \(S(1-s)=0\).

For a von Neumann subalgebra \(B\subset N\), set
\[
        \cH_B={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]

\begin{lemma}[induced subalgebra boundary]\label{lem:coeff}
For every expected subalgebra \(B\subset N\), \(X_{\cH_B}=X_B\), where the
left hand side is DKEP's coefficient boundary of the \(N\)-\(N\)
correspondence \(\cH_B\), and \(X_B\) is the subalgebra boundary of DKEP
Example 3.4.  In the tracial case
\(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\) as \(N\)-\(N\) correspondences.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and \(K={}_BL^2N_N\).  DKEP Example 5.11 gives
\(X_H=X_B\).  For the vector \(\widehat1\in H\), the operator
\(T_{\widehat1}:L^2N\to L^2B\) is \(E_B\), so
\(T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\widehat1}=e_B\) and
\(T_{\widehat1}T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}=1_{L^2B}\).  Thus \(H\) is mixing relative
to \(X_B\times \mathbb B(L^2B)\).  For \(\widehat1\in K\),
\(T_{\widehat1}:L^2B\to L^2N\) is the inclusion, so
\(T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\widehat1}=1_{L^2B}\); hence \(K\) is mixing relative
to \(\mathbb B(L^2B)\times Y_K\), where \(Y_K\) is its right coefficient
boundary.  DKEP Theorem 5.10 and the fusion identity
\(T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\) give that
\(H\otimes_BK=\cH_B\) is mixing relative to \(X_B\times Y_K\).  By the
minimality in DKEP Example 5.11, \(X_{\cH_B}\subset X_B\).

Conversely, if \(\xi,\eta\) are bounded in \(H\), then
\(\xi\otimes\widehat1\) and \(\eta\otimes\widehat1\) are bounded in
\(\cH_B\), and, since \(T_{\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\widehat1}=1_{L^2B}\) for
\(\widehat1\in K\),
\[
 T_{\xi\otimes\widehat1}^{\,*}T_{\eta\otimes\widehat1}
 =T_\xi^{\,*}T_\eta .
\]
Thus every coefficient generator of \(X_H=X_B\) belongs to \(X_{\cH_B}\).
The map \(\overline{\xi\otimes\eta}\mapsto J\eta\otimes J\xi\) gives the
stated self-conjugacy.
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\subset V\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\), hence
\(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\).

\begin{lemma}[direct sums and finite coefficient algebras]\label{lem:dsupport}
Let \(X\subset\mathbb B(L^2N)\) be a boundary piece with
\(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\).  If
\(\cK=\bigoplus_{j\in J}\cK_j\) is an \(N\)-\(N\) correspondence and
\(X_{\cK_j}\subset X\) for every \(j\), then every coefficient of \(\cK\)
belongs, after the canonical embedding
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\subset\cA_N\), to \(q_X\cA_Nq_X\).

For \(U\subset V\), let \(\cF_U\) be the class of non-empty finite Connes
fusions of copies of
\(\cH_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\) and \(\overline{\cH_U}\).  Define
\[
 \mathcal C_U=\operatorname{span}\{\,T_\xi^*T_\eta:
       \xi\in\cK,\ \eta\in\cL \hbox{ bounded},\ \cK,\cL\in\cF_U\,\}.
\]
Then \(\mathcal C_U\) is a self-adjoint algebra, and \(X_{M_U}\) has a
contractive approximate unit contained in the norm closure of \(\mathcal C_U\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}

\(X\times\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  Let \(H_0\) be the set of bounded vectors
\(\xi\in\cK\) for which \(L_\xi\in B_N(L^2N,\cK)\) is

summands lie in \(H_0\).  If \(\xi_n\in H_0\) and \(\xi_n\to\xi\) in Hilbert
```
